coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and application of novel sodium carboxy methyl cellulose-g-poly acrylic acid carbon dots hydrogel nanocomposite (NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs) for adsorptive removal of malachite green dye

coredata.dc:description:
This study aimed on synthesizing novel sodium carboxy methyl cellulose-g-poly acrylic acid carbon dots hydrogel nanocomposite (NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs) to remove malachite green (MG) dye from aqueous media. The characterisation of functionalities, morphology, particle size and crystallography of NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite both before and after adsorption. The results of the characterization analysis revealed the presence of numerous ionic functionalities such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups in nanocomposite. The morphological study revealed the presence of porosity, heterogenous and amorphous nature of adsorbent favoring the adsorption of MG. Furthermore, elemental analysis also confirmed the presence of carbon and oxygen as main elemental composition of nanocomposite. The temperature of 30 °C and the nanocomposite (having
pH
PZC
of 3) dose of 0.06 g at pH 10 results in achieving 96.92 % of MG dye removal. The experimental data fits closely with pseudo-second and Freundlich models. The enhanced MG dye adsorption by NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs was attributed to different mechanisms such as electrostatic or H-bonding as well as pi-pi interactions. Thermodynamically, the studied process was spontaneous and is of endothermic kind. Overall, the study reveals adsorptive property of novel NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite as a promising adsorbent for MG dye adsorption from water.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
This study aimed on synthesizing novel sodium carboxy methyl cellulose-g-poly acrylic acid carbon dots hydrogel nanocomposite (NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs) to remove malachite green (MG) dye from aqueous media. The characterisation of functionalities, morphology, particle size and crystallography of NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite both before and after adsorption. The results of the characterization analysis revealed the presence of numerous ionic functionalities such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups in nanocomposite. The morphological study revealed the presence of porosity, heterogenous and amorphous nature of adsorbent favoring the adsorption of MG. Furthermore, elemental analysis also confirmed the presence of carbon and oxygen as main elemental composition of nanocomposite. The temperature of 30 °C and the nanocomposite (having
pH
PZC
of 3) dose of 0.06 g at pH 10 results in achieving 96.92 % of MG dye removal. The experimental data fits closely with pseudo-second and Freundlich models. The enhanced MG dye adsorption by NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs was attributed to different mechanisms such as electrostatic or H-bonding as well as pi-pi interactions. Thermodynamically, the studied process was spontaneous and is of endothermic kind. Overall, the study reveals adsorptive property of novel NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite as a promising adsorbent for MG dye adsorption from water.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution is a pressing global concern, with increasing research interest in addressing this issue [1,2]. Industrial wastewater, often contaminated with various pollutants, poses significant environmental hazards [3]. Effective water purification is vital for maintaining the quality of our water resources [4]. Dyes, a group of organic compounds widely used in industries like textiles, are major contributors to water pollution. Untreated industrial effluents containing dyes can adversely affect water quality, leading to unpleasant odors, tastes, and visual impacts [5,6]. One of the cationic dyes i.e., malachite green (MG) dye, find extensive applications in industries [7], however, inspite of its beneficial uses, this dye known to be highly toxic to all life forms [8–10]. This highlights the need for MG dye removal from polluted water [11]. In view of this context, various water treatment techniques including membrane filtration [12], photodegradation [13], oxidation [14] and adsorption methods [15–19] were developed by the researchers. Among these techniques, adsorption method is the most efficient process due to simple operation and easy availability of adsorbents [14–16,18,19–22]. In adsorption process, when it comes to the selection of the adsorbents based on their economic as well as the technical requirements then the polysaccharide materials attract researcher’s attention as an efficient material for the development of adsorbents. Their eco-friendly nature, simple synthesis and the sustainable abundant reserves [23] make them efficient materials for use as an adsorbent.

Among different types of the polysaccharides [24–28], cellulose is one of the most efficient adsorbent owing to its remarkable degradability. Nevertheless, poor solubility in water limit its uses for efficient adsorption process. It is for this reason, modification/ fabrication of the cellulose with some other polymeric materials carried out. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a characteristic derivative of the cellulose having carboxymethyl groups that are attached to some of -OH groups present on the cellulose backbone [29]. CMC when reacted with sodium (Na), it results in the formation of the Na-CMC that is an eco-friendly, non-toxic in nature with a low-cost of production. Hydrogel networks, composed of NaCMC and PAAc, offer a promising approach for dye removal. These materials are known for their non-toxicity, environmental friendliness, and strong adsorption capabilities [30]. Their three-dimensional structure and functional groups (e.g., amide, carboxylic, hydroxyl) enable effective interaction with dye molecules. This property, coupled with high-water absorption capacity of polyacrylic acid (PAA) that exhibit exceptional water absorption capacity, often swelling to several times their original weight. This characteristic makes them a cornerstone of superabsorbent materials, which are widely used in various applications, including adsorption processes as adsorbents [31].

Keeping in view the above discussion, for treating the dye loaded polluted water, Na-CMC has been employed as a dye adsorbent by many researchers [32]. In addition to Na-CMC, poly acrylic acid (PAAc) exhibit remarkable adsorption efficiency [33] due to the availability of the numerous functionalities such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxylic acid as well as sulfonic acid groups [34]. Carbon dots (CDs) are also extensively employed as an adsorbent for water treatment. These are dimensionless carbon nanoparticles (NPs) having the size > 10 nm [35]. It is observed that the carbon of CDs is an outstanding adsorbent for dyes adsorption [36]. Although, the polymeric materials possess variety of uses in different applications [37], nevertheless, low solubility and low level processability limit their uses on a wider scale. This leads the researchers to focus on combining the polymers to synthesize different forms of the composites or nanocomposites [38] for dyes removal [39–44].

Keeping in consideration the above discussion, this research, for the first time, is devoted to the synthesis of the novel sodium carboxy methyl cellulose-g-poly acrylic acid carbon dots hydrogel nanocomposite (NaCMC-g-PAAc/CDs) by the free radical polymerization method. The successful synthesis of the nanocomposite was informed by different characterization techniques including Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (EDS), Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Furthermore, swelling % ratio and point of zero charge (
pH
PZC
) determination of prepared nanocomposite was also carried out. The adsorptive potential of NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite towards malachite green (MG) dye was investigated in batch mode. Additionally, data from the experiments was investigated by different isothermal, kinetic models and the thermodynamic study to better understand the adsorption process and mechanism. Further, this study includes investigation by different error analysis models including Hybrid, Marquardt’s percent standard deviation (MPSD), the sum of absolute errors (EABS), the sum of squares of errors (ERRSQ), and average relative error (ARE) models (these models are explained in the Table File as a supplementary material under sub-heading
i on page 5) to validate the fitted model to data. Although many researchers worked on the synthesis of composites/ nanocomposites using hydrogel, CDs and polymeric materials [45–49], however, the NaCMC-g-PAAc/CDs nanocomposite displays excellent dye adsorption ability in an eco-friendly, inexpensive, workable nanocomposite formulation. The research introduces a novel NaCMC-g-PAAc/CDs nanocomposite, which has not been previously reported for the removal of malachite green dye. Moreover, the process adopted here will facilitate the designing of an efficient batch industrial plant to purge malachite green dye from water. In addition to applying different isothermal and kinetic models, this study also involves the use of five different error analysis models and explore the mechanism of MG dye removal as well.

Analytical grade reagents were employed as obtained without any further purification. Acrylic acid was sourced from Himdia while calcium chloride, hydrochloric acid (37 % w/w), potassium persulfate, sodium carboxy methyl cellulose and sodium chloride were procured from Fluka. From B.D.H and Sigma Aldrich, sodium hydroxide and N, N'-Methylene-bis-acrylamide were obtained respectively. Malachite green dye was sourced from Merck while from sdFluka, potassium chloride was obtained. For solution preparation, deionized water was used throughout the research.

Carbon dots synthesis was carried out by using red beet juice i.e., by mixing 100 g of red beet in 250 mL water followed by heating and cooling. The cooled material thus obtained was first filtered to remove the impurities and then placed in a stainless-steel autoclave, which was heated in an oven at 150 °C for 5 h. Resulting dark brown product then allowed to cool down at room temperature and washed several times with water to remove impurities and attaining a neutral pH in the filtrate. The substance thus obtained was separated by centrifuging it at 4000 rpm for 15 min and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to ensure complete drying. Fig. 1 outlines the preparation steps of the carbon dots undertaken in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sodium carboxy methyl cellulose-g-poly acrylic acid carbon dots hydrogel nanocomposite (NaCMC-g-PAAc/CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels embedded with NaCMC-g-PAAc were synthesized by free-radical copolymerization process in an aqueous solution, involving the dissolution of sodium carboxy methyl cellulose (NaCMC) at variable doses of 0.25 g, 0.5 g, 0.75 g and 1 g in 25 mL of distilled water. This solution was then kept in three-necked flask connected to a condenser, a separatory funnel, and a nitrogen gas supply while immersed in water bath. Solution was stirred continuously for complete dissolution over a period of 1 h to which 0.05 g of the initiator potassium persulfate (KPS), in 2 mL water, was added with constant stirring. Subsequently, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5 and 4.5 mL of acrylic acid and properly weighed 0.025, 0.05, 0.075 and 0.1 g of crosslinker N, N′ methylene bisacrylamide (MBA) were introduced through separatory funnel with constant stirring in presence of nitrogen gas at 70 °C for 4 h. This resulted in the formation of the hydrogel which was then cut into small pieces, followed by washing while stirring for 6 h. During washing process, the water was replaced every half-hour to remove the unreacted substances, followed by drying in an oven at 70 °C till the weight of embedded hydrogel become constant. The same procedure was repeated with the addition of different weights of carbon dots (CDs) i.e., 0.004, 0.008, 0.012 and 0.016 g dissolved in 2 mL of distilled water by using an ultrasonication path for 1 h as illustrated in Fig. 2. The adsorbent material was synthesized at an initial pH of 4.3 using acrylic acid as a key component. This acidic pH environment is crucial for the formation of the polymer network. Following the synthesis, the adsorbent was thoroughly washed to neutralize the pH to 7. The use of an acidic pH during the preparation process is essential for ensuring the desired structural and functional properties of the adsorbent.
The adsorbents i.e., carbon dots (CDs), hydrogel and the NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite were characterized by different techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, FESEM, XRD, TGA, TEM, EDS, AFM, pHpzc, swelling studies
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of prepared adsorbents was studied from 400 cm-1-4000 cm-1 [50] using Shimadzu, Japan, 8400 s Spectrophotometer for determining functionalities present in material [14,51,52]. The surface morphology of the adsorbents was examined by FESEM analysis [53] by using MIRA3, MIRA3, TESCAN, China (accelerating voltage = 25 kV). For crystallographic study [54,55], XRD analysis was carried out using XRD-6000, Shimadzu, Japan at an angular range of 2θ (10–80°). TGA of prepared adsorbents was carried out for measuring thermal stability [56] with TGA4000, Perkin Elmer, USA. The analysis of shape and particle size distribution of the materials [57,58] was carried out via TEM study using 912AB, Leo, Germany while EDS analysis was used for elemental study [59,60], by MIRA3, TESCAN, China. AFM study via AA3000, Angstrom Advanced Inc., USA was carried out for determining adsorbent surface texture [61], using AA3000, Angstrom Advanced Inc., USA. Furthermore, using a Decible pH meter (model pH-3110, Germany), pH of dye solutions was measured. All instruments used in this study were calibrated before commencing relevant measurements.

For determination of the point of zero charge of nanocomposite, a 20 mL of 500 mg/l of NaCl solution, maintained at 25 °C, was taken into seven 100 mL conical flasks. The solution pH adjusted within range 2 to 10 by using 0.1 M NaOH and 0.1 M HCl. After that, an accurately weighed 0.05 g of the prepared nanocomposite was then introduced to each solution. These flasks were then placed in a shaker and subjected to an agitation at a speed of 120 rpm for 24 h. This was followed by the pH measurement of the supernatant with the help of pH meter. The relationship between the final pH (pHf) and the initial pH (pHi) of solutions then plotted (with initial pH of each solution on x-axis and change in pH on y-axis). The point where these values intersect on the graph correspond to the
pH
PZC
of the NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs. The method used for zero-point charge determination was adapted from [62]. Swelling experiments on the NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs were conducted by immersing 0.5 g of the nanocomposite in 500 mL solutions having varying pH levels (i.e., 2 to 9) for a duration of 60 h. Following the immersion period, the nanocomposite was then placed on a filter paper to remove additional water and weighed again. Swelling ratios were subsequently calculated according to the specified Eq. (1):
(1)

represents the swollen and dry weight (g) of nanocomposite respectively.

Experiments were conducted to study the effect of time, pH, amount of hydrogel (NaCMC), carbon dots dosage, effect of nanocomposite, dye concentration as well as temperature. A diluted concentration at 100 mg/l of MG dye prepared from stock solution. In a calibration curve, limit of detection (LOD) was used to identify the minimum concentration that can be distinguished from the background. It is typically calculated as three times the standard deviation of the blank signal. Experiments carried out with a 10 mL solution having pH of 6.6 at 25 °C temperature (unless otherwise specified). Regarding NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs dosage, 0.05 g was used for all experiments. The equilibrium time used for MG dye adsorption was 120 min. Followed by adsorption, solution was centrifuged using an EBA 20 centrifuge (Hettich, Germany) at a speed of 6000 revolutions per minute (rpm) for 15 min at the University of Al-Qadisiyah. The supernatant solution was analysed for measuring residual dye concentration using the UV–visible spectrophotometer Shimadzu UV 1800, Japan (190 nm-1100 nm at λmax of 618 nm. Afterwards, percentage removal (%R) of MG dye and adsorption capacity (mg/g) of the NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs were calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3), correspondingly [63]:

All experiments were performed at normal room temperature with relative standard deviation (RSD) of ± 0.291 unless otherwise specified.

Three isotherm models i.e., Langmuir, Freundlich model and Temkin isotherm were applied to data for better understanding of adsorption behaviour and its type. Langmuir model concerned mainly with mono-layer adsorption taking place on specified number of active sites available on the surface of the adsorbent. According to this model, adsorption of the adsorbate on adsorbent surface is mainly governed by the chemical interactions [56]. The mathematical expression of this model expressed in Eq. (8):

According to this model, decrement in adsorption heat of all adsorbate molecules, that are adsorbed on the adsorbent surface, doesn’t occur logarithmically, relatively, a linear decrement in adsorption heat take place with the surface coverage of adsorbent. For investigating nature and mechanism of adsorption, thermodynamic factors including change in Gibbs free energy, ∆ G (kJ/mol), enthalpy, ∆ H (kJ/mol) as well as entropy, ∆ S, (J/mol K) were calculated. For calculation of
∆ G
(Eq. (13)), value of distribution coefficient,
k

, was used which is given in Eq. (14):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional group (FTIR) study before and after adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: Results of the FTIR study of carbon dots (CDs), NaCMC-g-PAAc and NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite, both before as well as after adsorption, are outlined in Fig. 3a-b, revealing presence of the numerous functionalities in each sample. The FTIR results of CDs (Fig. 3a) showed the presence of -OH group vibrations, obvious by peaks obtained from 3787.93 cm-1 to 2923.88 cm-1 [65]. Furthermore, the peaks obtained from 1704.95 cm-1 to 1550.66 cm-1 revealed C–H vibrations. Peaks obtained from 1488.94 cm-1 to 1100 cm-1 and at 2360.78 cm-1 corresponds to vibrations of C–O–C bond. Stretching vibrations of CC and CO bonds obvious from peaks obtained at 1064.63 cm-1 and at 871.76 cm-1 respectively. Additionally, CC bond vibrations of the unsaturated structure can be revealed by the peak obtained at 617.17 cm-1 [66]. When examining FTIR spectra of hydrogel (Fig. 3a), it was obvious that the peaks obtained at nearly 1697.24 cm-1, 1157.20 cm-1 and 918.05 cm-1, 671.18 cm-1 corresponds to the presence of COO- (unsymmetrical stretching vibrations of CO), C-O-C and C-O correspondingly. Peaks obtained at 1527.51 cm-1 and 1458.08 cm-1 is an indication of the presence of COO- (CO symmetrical stretching) [67,68]. The broadband peak obtained within the region 3625.92 cm-1 to 2360.70 cm-1 mainly due to O–H vibrations of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CMC [69].

From the FTIR results of the prepared nanocomposite before adsorption (Fig. 3b), the presence of the COO- functional group was obvious by the peaks obtained at approximately 1650.95 cm-1. The peaks obtained within the region from 2823.59 cm-1 to 2993.31 cm-1 and from 1481.22 cm-1 to 756.04 cm-1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of C−H and C−O bonds, respectively. However, post adsorption results of the nanocomposite (Fig. 3b) revealed variations in the peaks of the hydroxyl and carboxyl group both in terms of the area and peak intensity highlighting their involvement in MG dye adsorption [44].

FESEM study of CDs and the prepared nanocomposite highlighted in Figs. 4a and 4b, correspondingly. Study revealed presence of large-sized particles and less heterogeneous surface with particle length ranging from 241.06 nm to 649.54 nm for CDs, and small-sized particles with length between 28.87 nm to 34.88 nm for NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs, respectively. It was observed from the results that the CDs possess large sized particles however after the formation of the nanocomposite, the size of the particles reduced that results in increasing its surface area. Furthermore, roughness and heterogeneous surface of the prepared nanocomposite also increased when compared with the CDs. This surface texture is characterized by irregularities, indicative of multiple active sites distributed across its surface for adsorption [70].

Crystallographic results of carbon dots (CDs), NaCMC-g-PAAc and NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs are outlined in the Fig. 5 and further summarized in Table 1. The study revealed the crystalline nature of the CDs due to peak at 2θ = 20.36°. The amorphous nature of NaCMC-g-PAAc was indicated by the peak at 2θ = 20.43° in the results. Furthermore, the nanocomposite peak showing its amorphous nature appeared at 2θ = 20.83° [71]. Since no additional XRD peak was observed in the NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite, it was revealed that the nanocomposite was synthesized without deformation in the amorphous structure of the hydrogel [72].

The EDS results of the study provided information on the elemental composition of the carbon dots (CDs) [59]. Results are summarized in Fig. 6 that are further elaborated in
Tables 2–3. Results of the study informed the presence of C, O, Si and Ca as main elemental composition of CDs with % composition of 58.51 %, 38.18 %, 0.35 % and 2.96 % (by wt%) respectively [73,74]. Each peak of the EDS spectrum is specific to an atom corresponding to single element. The highest peak in obtained indicates the most abundant element of the CDs [66]. Quantitative and atomic identification results of CDs are summarized in Tables 2–3.

The surface properties of the materials studied, including carbon dots (CDs), NaCMC-g-PAAc, and NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs were characterized by AFM analysis, and the data was presented as 3D image as summarized in Fig. 7 a-c. The study revealed the porous structure of each adsorbent, which facilitates adsorption of MG dye [66]. The 3D images of AFM analysis revealed the two distinct regions present on the adsorbent surface. These two regions include bright region and the dark region. Among these, the bright area mainly corresponds to the highest points of the amorphous region while on the other hand, the darker area represents the presence of the pores or voids in the nanocomposite [75]. Furthermore, the study highlights that the maximum height of NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs was higher when compared with the raw carbon dots (CDs) and NaCMC-g-PAAc. These findings confirmed the successful formation of the nanocomposite with a high degree of porosity [61,75]. In addition to AFM analysis, the nanocomposite was further characterized by TEM analysis. This analysis helped in determining the shape and particle size distribution of the nanocomposite [57]. The Results for NaCMC and NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs are elaborated in Fig. 8 a-c, showing that hydrogel particles embedded in polymer hydrogel matrix. The surface texture of the nanocomposite was highly rough and heterogeneous. These findings highlight the successful synthesis of nanocomposite, valuable insights into the structural characteristics of nanocomposite and their potential impact on its adsorption performance towards MG dye [73].

The thermal stability of carbon dots (CDs), NaCMC-g-PAAc and NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite was investigated through TGA analysis at variable temperature intervals. Results are revealed in Fig. 9 a-c with supplementary Table 4. For CDs (Fig. 9 a), within the temperature range 39.55 to 235.12 oC, mass loss of 4.301 % was obtained that was then increased and reached to 36.130 % when the temperature was further increased to 616.09 oC. However, by further up surging the temperature to 757.10 oC, an additional mass loss of 9.133 % was obtained. When compared with the thermal behaviour of the hydrogel (Fig. 9 b), it was observed that the initial mass loss of 16.308 % was obtained by upsurging temperature from 39.97 oC to 388.33 oC and mass loss of 58.070 % was obtained by upsurging temperature from 388.33 oC to 498.99 oC. Increasing temperature further till 661.3 oC results in the mass loss of 3.77 %. These results of the TGA analysis conformed that CDs thermal stability is greater than hydrogel that was further corroborated by the crystalline nature of the CDs (sub-
Section 3.2 ). For nanocomposite (Fig. 9 c), mass loss of the 13.53 %, 80.05 %, and 5.385 % were obtained within the temperature ranges of 39.84–253.01 oC, 253.01–488.45 oC and 488.45–673.06 oC respectively. Thermal stability of nanocomposite was lower than that of CDs due to its amorphous nature. The initial mass loss for each sample can be attributed with the adsorbed water removal with slow rate [76]. In the next stage of sample heating, weight loss for each material might take place mainly by the dehydration of the monomers and the depolymerization process while the loss in weight at the third stage might be due to the reduction of volatile hydrocarbon or degradation to its oxidation form [77].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration of the malachite green (MG) dye solution
ce:section.ce:para:
To establish a calibration curve for malachite green (MG) dye, a solution with a concentration of 800 ppm was prepared by dissolving 0.8 g dye in sufficient amount of water to make volume of 1000 mL. Different dilute solutions having variable concentrations (1 mg/l to 18 mg/l) prepared. UV–vis spectrophotometer was then used for the measurement of absorbance of each dilute solution at the λmax of 618 nm as shown in Fig. 10 a. Afterwards, a calibration curve was thus plotted (Fig. 10 b and
Table 5) against the studied dye concentrations using the prepared spike solution of dye having known concentration. Results of the study showed the regression coefficient (R2) value of 0.994 revealing the constant molar absorptivity value over a wide range of concentrations being studied.

For investigating effect of hydrogel dose on adsorptive potential of composite for MG dye removal, the weight of hydrogel was varied from 0.25 g to 1.0 g for composite (NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs) preparation. The amount of the prepared composite used was 0.05 g. It is clear ( Fig. 11) that with an increase in hydrogel dose from 0.25 g to 0.75 g, there was an increase in both percentage removal and adsorption capacity afterwards a decrement was observed. Increasing the hydrogel dose from 0.25 g to 0.75 g results in increasing the % adsorption of MG dye from 92.70 % to 94.02 % respectively. However, further increase in hydrogel dose from 0.75 g to 1 g results in decreasing the % removal from 94.02 % to 92.95 % respectively. Furthermore, adsorption efficiency of studied composite increases from 18.54 mg/g to 18.80 mg/g when the hydrogel dose was increased from 0.25 g to 0.75 g respectively. With increasing hydrogel dose to 1 g, a decrement in adsorption capacity was observed to 18.59 mg/g ( Table 6). The initial increment in the removal % of MG dye with increasing hydrogel loading was attributed to the introduction of the ionic functionalities (such as hydroxyl or carboxyl groups of the hydrogel) to the prepared nanocomposite that aid in MG dye adsorption [23]. Additionally, an increase in hydrogel dose to the nanocomposite results in increasing the synergistic effects between the hydrogel and the CDs in the prepared nanocomposite [24]. However, % of dye removal starts decreasing after certain amount of hydrogel that possibly attributed to the declined synergistic effects of the hydrogel and the CDs in the prepared nanocomposite [78]. Overall, the study revealed that a lower NaCMC dose (0.75 g) had better performance in improving the adsorptive potential of the prepared composite. Results of the study are consistent with the results obtained by [44].

Effect of CDs on adsorption efficiency of the prepared composite (NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs) was studied from 0.004 g to 0.016 g. The amount of the resulting composite used for the experiment was 0.05 g. Findings of the study are shown in Fig. 12 and Table 7 . The study revealed that by increasing the CDs dose from 0.004 g to 0.008 g, there was a continuous increase in % adsorption of MG dye from 94.93 % to 95.27 % respectively afterwards it starts decreasing to 94.73 % while using 0.016 g of the CDs dose. Furthermore, in terms of the adsorption capacity, it was observed increasing CDs dose from 0.004 g to 0.008 g, a continual increment in
q
e
mg
g
from 18.98 mg/g to 19.05 mg/g respectively afterward it decreases to 18.94 mg/g when 0.016 g of CDs dose was used. This was due to reason that an increase in CDs dose results in increasing synergistic effects between the hydrogel and the CDs in the prepared nanocomposite [24] but upto a certain limit. While on the other hand, a very higher CDs dose limits the MG dye adsorption by the agglomeration on the available active sites of nanocomposite thereby reducing synergistic effect and hence the % removal of the dye. It is for this reason, CDs dose of 0.008 g was used as the optimum dose for the composite preparation.

Fig. 13 and Table 8 showed that increasing pH from 3 to 10, there was a continuous increase in the % removal and maximum adsorption capacity (mg/g) of MG dye from 88.79 % to 95.94 % and from 17.75 mg/g to 19.18 mg/g respectively. Lower MG removal in acidic pH can be attributed to protonation of functionalities of nanocomposite resulting in introducing positive charge on its surface. Positively charged surface thereby facilitates electrostatic repulsions between the cationic MG dye molecules and the cationic nanocomposite surface. While on the other, at basic pH value, due to the presence of the hydroxyl ions in the solution, the carboxyl/ amino groups of the nanocomposite exist in their anionic form (by the deprotonation process) thereby imparting the negative charge on the nanocomposite surface. This negatively charged nanocomposite surface then results in electrostatic interactions between the cationic dye molecules and the anionic nanocomposite surface thereby favoring the adsorption process [14]. In general, the results of the study revealed the remarkable effects of the solution pH on the MG dye removal by adsorption on the studied nanocomposite.

For the better understanding of the pH effect on MG dye adsorption, an adsorbent characteristic termed as point of zero charge (
pH
PZC
) was determined. This
pH
PZC
refers to the pH where the surface of the adsorbent is neutral i.e., has net charge of 0. The surface of the adsorbent acquires positive or negative charge when the solution pH was ˂
pH
PZC
or >
pH
PZC
due to the protonation and the deprotonation of the adsorbent’s functional groups respectively [14]. The results of the study are outlined in Fig. 14 and Table 9 . It is clear from the study that the
pH
PZC
of the prepared nanocomposite was 3 suggesting that the surface of the adsorbent become positively charged when the solution pH is < 3 while the surface became negatively charged when pH is ≥ 3. This suggests that the solution pH greater than 3 favors the uptake of the cationic MG dye molecules due to the electrostatic interactions between the cationic dye molecules and the anionic nanocomposite surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on swelling percentage of the nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The variations in the swelling ratio (swelling %) of the nanocomposite with change in solution pH were investigated. The results of the study are presented in Fig. 15 and are further elaborated in Table 10 . The study revealed that with an increase in solution pH from 2 to 9, the swelling ratio (%) of the nanocomposite increased from 206 % to 8490 %. The effect can be explained by the fact that at an alkaline pH, the ionization of –COOH groups of the prepared nanocomposite take place, and the H-bonds present in these carboxyl groups starts weakening slowly [79]. This results in the electrostatic repulsion between the polymer chains of the studied adsorbent giving rise to the additional volume being accessible in the prepared nanocomposite to retain extra/ absorbed water thereby leading to an increase in the swelling capacity. In acidic medium, however, the decrease in the swelling percentage might be due to the protonation of the carboxylate ions of the nanocomposite leading to the reduction in the anion-anion repulsive forces. Additionally, the effect of the protonation results in the strengthening of the H-bonds present in the –COOH group of the nanocomposite thereby improving the degree of the physical crosslinking in the skeleton network of the prepared nanocomposite and reducing the swelling ratio [80,81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs hydrogel nanocomposite dose
ce:section.ce:para: The quantity of the NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs hydrogel nanocomposite was varied from 0.01 g to 0.06 g to investigate its effect on MG dye adsorption. Results of the study ( Fig. 16 and Table 11) revealed an increase in % removal with respect to nanocomposite dose. A removal of 93.57 % and 95.03 % could be obtained at the nanocomposite dose of 0.01 g and 0.06 g respectively. This increase in MG removal with nanocomposite dose may be due to presence of free active sites adsorbent [19,82]. However, the adsorption capacity shows a continuous decrement from 93.57 mg/g to 15.83 mg/g with an increase in composite dose from 0.01 g to 0.06 g. This behaviour was due to reason that with an initial increase in nanocomposite dose, nearly all active sites got filled by the dye molecules leaving behind a smaller number of active sites accessible for adsorption [83].

Results of contact time study are outlined in Fig. 17. Study showed that% removal and adsorption capacity exhibit an increment with an upsurge in time till 120 min. At time of 1 min and 120 min, 52.47 % and 94.12 % of MG removal take place with adsorption capacity of 5.24 mg/g and 9.41 mg/g correspondingly ( Table 12). Distinct increase in removal rates with an increase in time can be due to higher chances of interaction between MG dye molecules and the prepared nanocomposite [84,85]. Nevertheless, when time was increased from 120 min to 180 min, % removal and the adsorption efficiency of the studied nanocomposite were not increased to a remarkable extent as 94.22 % MG removal and 9.42 mg/g of adsorption capacity was obtained after 180 min. In other words, extending the contact time to 180 min resulted in only a slight increase in MG removal, suggesting that the system had essentially stabilized. Therefore, in order to conserve time and energy, the subsequent experiments were conducted at 120 min

Fig. 18 shows how concentration of MG dye affects removal of NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs hydrogel nanocomposite at variable temperatures i.e., 15–30 °C. At 0 mg/L of MG concentration (at all studied temperatures), the adsorption capacity was zero. However, as the concentration of MG dye was increased from 50 mg/L to 700 mg/L, adsorption capacity increases steadily from 9.24 to 129.45 mg/g, from 9.272 to 131.42 mg/g, from 9.53 to 134.85 mg/g and from 9.73 to 135.69 mg/g at 15, 20, 25 and 30 °C correspondingly. Equilibrium was achieved at 30 °C when 700 mg/L of dye was used as listed in Table 13. This trend can be due to movement of dye molecules towards nanocomposite resulting in adsorbing dye on its surface rather than to be soluble in the solution resulting in its increased adsorptive potential at higher concentration. Results shows adsorption process takes place endothermically, as demonstrated by the positive ∆H value (Table 19) [86].

Results ( Fig. 19, Table 14) exposed gradual increase in both the percentage removal and the adsorption capacity with an increase in temperature. The highest removal observed at 30 °C, with 96.92 % MG adsorption. Conversely at 15 °C, only 92.47 % of MG removal take place. Further, increasing temperature from 15 °C to 30 °C results in an increase in adsorption capacity from 129.45 to 135.69 mg/g. Increased temperatures provide energy for dye molecules to get adsorbed on nanocomposite as well as to get diffuse into its inner part. Results revealed that the adsorption is favourable at elevated temperatures at which the dye molecules get adsorb on nanocomposite rather than staying in solution [87].

Results of the pseud first order model ( Fig. 20 and Table 15) indicate that regression coefficient value (R 2) is less than 1 i.e., 0.431 suggesting the unfitness of data to studied model. Additionally, there exist a greater difference between the values of the experimental
Q
e
(mg/g) i.e., 9.41 mg/g and the calculated
Q
e
(mg/g) i.e., 0.768 mg/g for this model. From the error analysis study i.e., Hybrid, MPSD, EABS, ERRSQ, and ARE (details of each error analysis model have been summarized in Table File under the subsection ‘
i' ), it was observed that higher error was associated with the pseudo-first-order model for all five studied error models unveiling a significant mismatch between experimental observations and model predictions ( Table 16). These results revealed the unfitness of this model to the experimental data.

Findings of the pseud second order model ( Fig. 21 and Table 15) of the study revealed R 2 of 0.999 (higher than that obtained with pseudo 1st order model). Furthermore, there is not significant variation between experimental
Q
e
(mg/g) i.e., 9.41 mg/g and the calculated
Q
e
(mg/g) i.e., 9.469 mg/g for this model. When the results of all error functions for pseudo-second model were compared with the pseudo-first model, it is clear that pseudo-second model has the lower error values of all studied models (Table 16). These findings of the study revealed the fitness of the experimental data to the pseudo-second-order model [19].

The results of the Langmuir isotherm model are represented in Fig. 22 and Table 17. The R 2 for Langmuir type 1, 2, 3 and 4 [64] were observed to be 0.351, 0.972, 0.455 and 0.513 respectively. By comparing the studied types of linear Langmuir model, it was observed that type 2 of Langmuir model fits best to data due to its higher regression coefficient value. However, from the highest error function across all the studied groups it is observed that data does not fit well to Langmuir model in describing MG dye adsorption on NaCMC-g-PAAc. The values obtained for the Langmuir separation factor (R
L ) comes out to be unity for revealing the linearity of the studied adsorption process. The maximum adsorption capacity (mg/g) for Langmuir type 1, 2, 3 and 4 were observed to be −285.71 mg/g, 3.92 mg/g, 40 mg/g and 0.026 mg/g respectively.

Results of Freundlich model are presented in Fig. 23 and Table 17 . It is clear from the study that the value of
R
2
comes out to be 0.974 which is the highest among all the studied isotherm models. From the analysis of the error models, it is clear that Freundlich model has the lowest error margins in comparison to the Langmuir and Temkin isotherm counterparts, as summarised in Table 18 . Furthermore, maximum adsorption capacity (mg/g) was 3.26 mg/g revealing compatibility of the Freundlich model to data.

Findings of Temkin model are elaborated in Fig. 24 and are further summarised in Table 17 . Results of the Temkin model offered regression coefficient value of 0.876 (lower in comparison to the values of Freundlich model but still higher than Langmuir model). Further, comparison of error analysis models revealed higher error margins for Temkin model than Freundlich model (lower in comparison to Langmuir model counterparts Table 18) highlighting the need of other models as well for understanding the adsorption process.

Table 19 compares the adsorption capacities of various adsorbents for MG dye removal. The comparison reveals that different adsorbents exhibit varying adsorption capacities. However, the prepared NaCMC-g-PAAc/CDs nanocomposite demonstrated competitive or superior performance compared to several other adsorbents. This highlights its potential as a promising adsorbent for MG dye removal.

For investigating nature and mechanism of adsorption, thermodynamic factors including change in Gibbs free energy, ∆ G (kJ/mol), enthalpy, ∆ H (kJ/mol) as well as entropy, ∆ S, (J/mol K) were calculated. The results of the thermodynamic study are shown in Fig. 25, as a Van’t Hoff plot, and are further summarized in Table 20. The data reveals the adsorption process as spontaneous and endothermic as evident from
− ∆ G
and
+ ∆ H
values. Furthermore, feasibility of adsorption is also confirmed by value obtained for
∆ S
[95].

The presence of polyacrylic acid (PAA), a negatively charged polymer, plays a crucial role in the adsorption of cationic malachite green dye. The electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged functional groups of PAA and the positively charged malachite green molecules facilitates the adsorption process. Additionally, the porous structure of the NaCMC-g-PAAc/CDs nanocomposite provides a large surface area for dye interaction, further enhancing the removal efficiency. The FTIR study of NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs further exposed presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on its surface. These groups can bind with dye molecules via ionic interactions between negative functionalities and positive dye molecules [14] as revealed in Fig. 26. However, in addition to the ionic interactions, MG dye molecules also diffuse into inner surface of nanocomposite and get adsorbed there via pore filling. Furthermore, π-π interactions between MG dye molecules and aromatic groups of nanocomposite [96], leading to maximum dye uptake from aqueous solution. Finally, due to hydrogen and ionic functionalities, some kinds of the H-bonding also developed that further aid in dye removal by adsorption. These mechanisms contribute to intricate adsorption of MG dye onto nanocomposite surface majorly by physical interactions, representing multifaceted nature of removing MG dye from polluted water.

This paper demonstrated the use of the novel NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite prepared by the free radical polymerization method as a promising alternative to malachite green (MG) dye removal from water. The
q
e
(mg/g) of NaCMC-g-PAAc/ CDs nanocomposite was found to be 3.26 mg/g after time of 120 min at pH 10. MG dye removal of 96.92 % was obtained under the optimized conditions, i.e., the hydrogel loading of 0.75 g using CDs dose of 0.008 g with 0.06 g of the prepared nanocomposite for 700 mg/L of dye concentration at 30 °C. Different characterisation techniques suggested that the prepared nanocomposite played an essential role in MG dye sorption, primarily controlled by the physical adsorption mechanism. The zero charge point of the nanocomposite was observed to be 3 and its swelling ratio (%) increased with an increase in solution pH. The adsorption mechanism for MG dye removal was determined through isothermal, kinetic and thermodynamic analyses. Kinetic and isothermal modelling exposed the intricate dynamics of adsorption, with pseudo-second and Freundlich model emerging as the most suitable descriptors. The thermodynamic study revealed the spontaneity and the endothermic nature of the process. Furthermore, the mechanistic study highlighted the multifaceted nature of MG dye adsorption, encompassing H-bonding, ionic and π-π interactions along with pore filling mechanisms.
